text: The surgeon sharply dissects the attachments between the colon and the abdominal wall, incising the white line of Toldt. The incision is made lateral to the mesentery of the colon and extended in both directions.

The surgeon identifies the mesentery of the colon, which appears as a slightly darker yellow compared to the surrounding perinephric fat. Using a grasper, the mesentery is gently elevated, and the fibroareolar attachments are sharply divided.

The surgeon identifies the plane between the intestinal mesentery and Girauda's fascia, which is relatively avascular and can be developed bluntly. A harmonic scalpel is used to divide the attachments, mobilizing the colon and spleen medially. The peanut retractor is carefully maneuvered to avoid puncturing the spleen.

The surgeon identifies the ureter at the lower pole of the kidney, observing its peristalsis to confirm its identity. The ureter is then retracted superiorly to protect it during the high-low dissection.
The surgeon identifies the gonadal vein inserting into the renal vein and continues dissection posterior to the renal vein to reveal the renal artery, which is visible just behind the renal vein.
The surgeon removes dense perinephric fat using a combination of harmonic dissection and blunt dissection techniques, as the fat does not mobilize easily with blunt dissection alone.

The surgeon uses laparoscopic ultrasound to define the tumor boundaries. A Satinsky clamp is applied to the hilum, and an additional 5mm trocar is inserted. The hilum is clamped, with the renal artery clamped while the vein remains open.

The surgeon uses sharp scissors to dissect around the tumor, sharply encircling the renal capsule to delineate its boundary. Minimal bleeding is observed due to the clamping of the renal artery. A 5mm trocar is utilized to position an assistant for tumor retraction, enhancing visibility. The surgeon manages both the suction and the scissors during the procedure.

The surgeon distinguishes normal renal parenchyma from the tumor, noting the grossly negative margin. The tumor is incised, revealing a texture distinctly different from the surrounding normal renal tissue.
The surgeon has successfully detached the tumor specimen from the kidney, indicating that the dissection has progressed beyond the tumor.

The surgeon uses CPX needles, which have been partially straightened, to insert through a twelve millimeter trocar below the entire tumor bed. Figure of eight sutures are placed carefully to cover the tumor bed, aiming to prevent bleeding and urine leaks.
The surgeon places a second figure-of-eight suture, stabilizing the kidney while inserting the needle into the tissue. Care is taken to avoid deep bites during the suturing process.

Laparotides are applied to secure the suture and achieve hemostasis. The surgeon releases the hilar clamp, noting a total warm ischemia time of twenty minutes. The tumor bed appears completely dry. The specimen, along with perinephric fat, is placed into a ten millimeter endocatch bag for pathology staging analysis.
